main_summary.summary: Gertrude Newsome Jackson (b. 1923, Madison, Illinois) recounts her work as a community organizer in Marvell, Arkansas (Phillips County) during the 1960s civil rights movement. After her family relocated to an 88-acre cotton farm in Arkansas following her grandfather's death, Jackson attended a one-room segregated school while white students rode buses to a well-equipped facility. Her organizing began when a newly built Black school suffered repeated sewage backups that the Marvell school board refused to address; her husband (called 'Hog' Jackson) led the community in pulling children out of school, the area's first boycott. SNCC field workers Howard Himmelbaum and Myrtle Glascoe lived with relatives and held weekly meetings at Jones Ridge that drew packed churches. Jackson and her husband endured a cross burning in their yard, an arson attack on their farm shop, sugar in their cotton picker's fuel tank, and a roadside shooting incident. The Jacksons served as named plaintiffs in the federal school-desegregation suit decided by Judge Owen Harris with attorney John Walker, which produced a single-bus integration order. Jackson later helped found the Boys Girls Adults Community Development Center (BGACDC) with Gale Thrower, building Head Start home-visit programs, housing units, and youth tutoring. Reverend Jerome Turner and grandson Theodore Eldridge Chilling provide supplementary testimony.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Jackson's testimony documents civil rights organizing in the rural Mississippi Delta region of eastern Arkansas (Phillips County, Marvell), a geography less represented in mainstream movement narratives than urban Alabama or Mississippi. Her account specifies SNCC's Arkansas Project field workers Howard Himmelbaum and Myrtle Glascoe by name, the local Black leadership network including Reverend Overtis Adams and John Hamilton, and the federal litigation chain (Judge Owen Harris, attorney John Walker) that produced the Marvell school-integration order.

The interview also captures the post-movement continuity of organizing work through the founding of BGACDC, providing a primary-source account of how Head Start partnerships, housing development through FHA, and community-center infrastructure were built by movement veterans in the 1970s and beyond. Supplementary testimony from Reverend Jerome Turner and grandson Theodore Eldridge documents intergenerational transmission of movement memory.

chapters.title: Birth and Move to Arkansas
chapters.summary: Jackson recounts her 1923 birth in Madison, Illinois, and her family's relocation to an 88-acre cotton farm in Arkansas after her grandfather's death. She describes six brothers and four sisters and the differences between Illinois schooling and the rural Arkansas one-room schoolhouse.

chapters.title: Segregated Schooling and Childhood Discrimination
chapters.summary: Jackson describes the one-room Arkansas schoolhouse serving roughly sixty children across eight grades while white children rode buses to a modern facility. She recounts her daughter's wish to be white in order to attend the pretty school with swings and seesaws, and a boy striking a white passenger who shouted slurs from a bus window.

chapters.title: Sewage Boycott and First Organizing
chapters.summary: Jackson describes the consolidation school built for six Black communities and the repeated sewage backups that flooded the bathrooms. After the Marvell school board dismissed complaints, her husband organized parents to keep their children home, the community's first boycott. A Little Rock sanitation inspection forced repairs.

chapters.title: SNCC Workers Arrive
chapters.summary: Jackson recounts meeting SNCC field workers Howard Himmelbaum and Myrtle Glascoe through neighbor Overtis Adams. Himmelbaum first stayed with Jackson's uncle until the proximity to a white-frequented store made the host nervous, then moved to her cousin Carrie Davis's home. Weekly Thursday-night meetings drew packed churches.
related_events: SNCC organizing in Phillips County Arkansas

chapters.title: Retaliatory Violence
chapters.summary: Jackson describes a cross burned in her yard and another at Reverend Masco Anderson's home, the arson of the family farm shop, sugar placed in the cotton picker's fuel tank, and a roadside shooting where a truck passenger fired across the hood of her car. She recounts her husband arming himself before driving through a white landowner's property after threats.

chapters.title: County Fair Boycott
chapters.summary: Jackson recounts the decision at a movement meeting, proposed by Reverend Anderson, to boycott the Phillips County fair simultaneously with the school boycott. The fair drew no crowd despite the Pine Bluff college choir's scheduled performance, and the fair lost money that year.

chapters.title: Community Center and Head Start
chapters.summary: Jackson describes her work transporting children to Head Start for Mid-Delta Community Service, then teaching home-start sessions in family homes. She recounts a landlord ordering a Head Start mother off his property over Martin Luther King associations, and the family later buying their own house. She traces the founding of BGACDC with Gale Thrower around 1977-78 to provide books, immunization access, and youth programming.

chapters.title: School Integration Lawsuit
chapters.summary: Jackson describes the federal school-desegregation case after the Marvell school district refused to implement second-phase integration for grades four through eight. She recounts the morning Black families planned to walk children to the white school and found trucks bumper-to-bumper around the building with armed men in rifle racks. Judge Owen Harris ordered single-bus integration; attorney John Walker required witnesses to substitute correct terms for racial slurs in testimony.
related_events: armed truck blockade at white school

chapters.title: BGACDC Operations Today
chapters.summary: Reverend Jerome Turner, youth and family director at BGACDC in Poplar Grove near Marvell, describes the center's four program areas: youth and family services, economic development including the Best Food in Town cafeteria, housing through BGACDC Manor (39 units) and standalone units, and administration. Programs include the Delta Reach and Teach afterschool tutorial, Girls Are Girls, and a USDA-FDA-partnered Delta Obesity Research Project.

chapters.title: Grandson's Testimony
chapters.summary: Theodore Eldridge Chilling, Jackson's grandson and a USDA Farm Service Agency employee, describes growing up on his grandparents' farm and the family storytelling tradition that conveyed movement-era events without victimhood framing. He reflects on how Jackson's recognition across Phillips County, from police to state representatives, reveals the reach of her community work.

chapters.title: Center's Regional Influence
chapters.summary: Reverend Jerome Turner closes by identifying community-development centers in Marianna (Lee County, Arkansas), Mississippi, and Louisiana that were influenced by BGACDC's model through the Foundation of Midsouth collaborative. He credits Mrs. Beatrice Shelby and Mrs. Jackson as ambassadors who traveled to promote the center's work.
related_events: Marianna Lee County Community Development Center

loc_video.caption: Gertrude Newsome Jackson oral history interview conducted by LaFleur Paysour in Marvell, Arkansas, 2010 November 22
